content:
*I slide my hand onto my boy’s shoulder as the reader stands, the light from the eastern windows laying a bright bar across the pew ahead of us. He’s half-fidget, half-listen; I lean down so my breath doesn’t carry past him.*

"Hey—listen for the verbs," I whisper. "What the sky is doing."

*The words come: The heavens declare the glory of God… day to day pours out speech… night to night reveals knowledge… their voice goes out through all the earth… in them He has set a tent for the sun. I tap that strip of light and let the line work on him. We don’t argue the room; we let the room preach.*

When the psalm sits down, I don’t add a hedge. I won’t wink at him and empty good words to make them safer. If he looks up at me, I give him the small true sentence a ten-year-old can carry:

"Poetry doesn’t mean ‘not real.’ It means God is telling it so you can feel it. The sky is talking right now. You can hear it if you look."

*After the benediction, we don’t rush the door. We step outside, heads tilted back into the blue, sun riding his path like it always does.*

"What did the sky say to you today?" I ask. "Show me with your finger where the sun came from this morning, where it’s going tonight."

If he asks, "Does it really talk without words?" I nod. "Like a river does, or a fire in the stove. You don’t need letters to know what it’s saying. God set it up that way so even kids can hear it."

Unity? I keep it by refusing to make my son the place where we blur the edges. I won’t pick a fight in the pews; I’ll take my elder to coffee midweek, open Psalm 19 on the table, and say, "Brother, I can soften my tone for the sake of peace, but I can’t teach my kid that ‘poetic’ means ‘optional.’ The psalm doesn’t let me. Let’s let the sky do its job, and we’ll do ours with gentleness."

*Back home at the table, I put a pencil in his hand and draw a simple circle of our town, an east mark and a west. He traces the sun’s run across the day, grinning at how straight a sermon can look when it lands on paper. We thank God for the sky’s voice over lunch, brief and glad, and we leave it there—no hedges, no fight, just the truth his ears can keep.*
